front: What is the primary goal of the Copy-on-Write (COW) technique?
back: The primary goal is to defer the actual copying of memory until it is absolutely necessary, thereby optimizing performance and memory usage by allowing processes to share the same physical memory pages initially.

front: Describe the initial state of memory when a new process is created using fork() with Copy-on-Write.
back: The parent and child processes initially share all the same physical pages of memory. These shared pages are marked as read-only. This sharing is transparent to the processes.

front: What event triggers an actual page copy in a Copy-on-Write system?
back: A page fault is triggered when either process (parent or child) attempts to write to a shared, read-only page. The operating system's page fault handler then intercepts this write attempt and performs the copy.

front: What are two significant performance benefits of using Copy-on-Write?
back: 1. Faster process creation (e.g., fork() becomes quicker as it avoids immediate, potentially large, memory copies). 2. Reduced memory consumption, as unchanged pages between parent and child processes are never duplicated.

front: Beyond the fork() system call, name another common application of the Copy-on-Write technique.
back: Copy-on-Write is also used for efficient memory-mapped files (mmap) with the MAP_PRIVATE flag, allowing multiple processes to map the same file read-only and only copy pages when a process modifies its private view.
